name: Chinle Wash
short_description: Free BLM rockhounding along a scenic wash near the Utah-Arizona border. Hunt for agate, jasper, and petrified wood in colorful Chinle Formation badlands with minimal crowding.
description: Chinle Wash offers accessible rockhounding on public BLM land in one of Utah's most geologically diverse regions. The wash cuts through badlands formed by the Chinle Formation, exposing layers of mudstone, sandstone, and volcanic ash that host colorful agates, jasper nodules, and petrified wood fragments. This area sees relatively light collector pressure compared to famous Utah rockhounding sites, making it ideal for finding intact specimens. The landscape here is rugged desert with exposed rock formations on both sides of the wash, providing ample opportunity to spot material in the wash floor and along eroded banks. Spring and fall offer the best collecting conditions when temperatures are moderate and flash flood risk is lower. Be prepared for a remote experience - bring plenty of water, a four-wheel-drive vehicle is recommended for access, and cell service is spotty at best.
history: The Chinle Wash region sits within the Chinle Formation, a Late Triassic sequence of fluvial and lacustrine deposits laid down 200+ million years ago when this area was a warm, wet floodplain. Silica-rich groundwater percolating through these ancient rocks created the agate and chalcedony concentrations found here today. Modern prospectors have worked the accessible washes in San Juan County since the 1960s uranium boom era, though most focus now is on collecting colorful specimens rather than ore minerals.
tips: Focus your search in the wash floor itself and along the eroded banks where water has exposed fresh material - look for translucent or banded agate pieces with red, orange, brown, and cream coloring. Bring a classifier or mesh bag to sift through gravels in the wash; many agates are golf-ball-sized or smaller here. Check areas where the wash widens or where side canyons feed in, as these are natural collection points for denser minerals. Use a 10x hand lens to spot chalcedony and jasper on the surface - color is your best guide, so scan for anything with unusual banding or bright hues against the grey mudstone.
terrain_notes: The wash sits at approximately 4,200 feet elevation in desert badlands terrain. Expect loose rocks, scattered boulders, and exposed clay banks. Walking is on uneven wash gravel with occasional soft sand. The surrounding landscape is sparsely vegetated with scattered pinyon-juniper. Parking areas are primitive pullouts; from parking you may need to hike 0.5 to 2 miles depending on where you access the wash. Footing is challenging but manageable for anyone in basic physical condition.
best_season: October - April. Winter temperatures in this high desert location are mild (30-50 degrees F), and early spring (March-April) offers comfortable collecting weather. Summer heat (90-110 degrees F) and afternoon thunderstorm risk make June-September challenging. Fall (October-November) is ideal with clear skies and cool mornings.
written_directions: From Monticello, Utah, take US 191 south toward Bluff for approximately 35 miles. Turn east onto Utah State Route 95 toward Natural Bridges National Monument. Continue on UT 95 for about 15 miles. Watch for unsigned BLM parking pullouts on both sides of the highway near washes. Chinle Wash crossings and access points are found intermittently along UT 95 in this region. Standard passenger vehicles can reach some upper wash areas, but a high-clearance or four-wheel-drive vehicle is recommended to reach lower productive sections. Roads to pullouts are rough dirt with washboard surface.
road_conditions: Rough dirt roads with washboard surface. High-clearance or 4WD recommended. Some pullouts accessible to passenger vehicles but main productive areas require 4x4.
parking_notes: Primitive BLM pullouts along UT 95 and wash approach roads. No developed parking facilities. Space for 3-5 vehicles at most pullouts. No amenities.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per Federal Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA). No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. Respect all boundary markers and posted closures. Leave the site cleaner than you found it.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
No vandalism of geological features
Pack out all trash
nearest_services: Monticello, Utah (approximately 45 miles northwest) has gas, food, lodging, and water. Bluff, Utah (approximately 35 miles west) offers basic services. No services within 20 miles of the wash.
accessibility_notes: Uneven, rocky wash terrain with loose gravel footing. Eroded banks are unstable. Scattered sharp rocks present. Steep slopes require good balance and stability. Not wheelchair accessible. Sturdy footwear mandatory. Hiking distance from parking to productive collecting areas ranges from 0.5 to 2 miles depending on access point. Limited mobility visitors should choose upper wash areas accessible closer to pullouts.

faq.question: What types of rocks and minerals can I find rockhounding at Chinle Wash Utah?
faq.answer: Chinle Wash is known for colorful agate, chalcedony, jasper, and petrified wood. Most specimens are found as nodules and fragments in the wash floor and along eroded banks of the Chinle Formation badlands. Agates here often display red, orange, cream, and brown banding. Jasper specimens tend to be opaque and range from 1 to 4 inches.

faq.question: Is rockhounding at Chinle Wash free and do I need a permit?
faq.answer: Yes, Chinle Wash is free BLM public land with no permit required for recreational rockhounding. You are limited to collecting 25 lbs per day for personal use only under FLPMA rules. No commercial collection is allowed.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound at Chinle Wash near Monticello?
faq.answer: October through April offers ideal collecting conditions. Fall and spring provide comfortable temperatures (40-70 degrees F), clear skies, and lower flash flood risk. Summer heat (90-110 degrees F) and afternoon monsoon thunderstorms make June-September challenging. Winter is mild but mornings can be cold.

faq.question: How do I find agate and jasper at Chinle Wash - what tools do I need?
faq.answer: Focus on the wash floor gravels and eroded banks where water has exposed material. Bring a 10x hand lens to spot banding and color on small pieces. A classifier or mesh bag helps sift material quickly. Carry a small pick or chisel for extracting specimens from clay banks. Most productive areas require hiking 0.5-2 miles from parking, so lightweight tools are best.

faq.question: Is Chinle Wash rockhounding site suitable for beginners and families with children?
faq.answer: Yes, Chinle Wash is beginner-friendly with material visible on the surface and in the wash floor. Kids age 8 and up can participate safely with supervision. The moderate difficulty rating reflects terrain hazards (loose rocks, loose banks, flash flood risk in wet season) rather than specimen rarity. Start in upper wash areas closer to parking.

faq.question: What should I know about visiting Chinle Wash - access roads, weather, and safety hazards?
faq.answer: A high-clearance or 4WD vehicle is recommended to access the most productive sections via rough dirt roads from UT 95 near Bluff. Cell service is spotpy. Bring 2-3 gallons of water per person, sun protection, and a first aid kit. Watch for flash flood danger April-September after rain. Remote location means no services within 20 miles, so plan accordingly.

meta_title: Chinle Wash Rockhounding - Utah Agate & Jasper
meta_description: Free rockhounding on BLM land near Bluff. Find agate, jasper, and petrified wood in scenic Chinle Wash badlands. No permit required, 25 lb daily limit.
